params.text:
Detecting fire early

The sooner a fire is detected, the more time there is to protect people. Because care homes are premises where people sleep, the law requires an automatic fire detection and alarm system – typically networked smoke detectors and heat detectors linked to a central control panel, backed up by manual call points (the red "break-glass" boxes) at exits and along corridors.

You should know, for your own workplace:

  • What the alarm sounds like and how it differs from the weekly test.
  • Where the nearest manual call points and fire exits are.
  • Where the fire alarm control panel is and who is trained to interpret it.

Alarms, detectors, emergency lighting and fire doors are only effective if they are maintained and tested regularly. If you notice a covered detector, a wedged-open fire door, a blocked exit or a fault indicator on the panel, report it straight away – never ignore it.

params.alt: A wall-mounted fire alarm call point

params.text:
Fire doors

Fire doors are a vital part of detection and containment. They are designed to hold back fire and smoke for a set period, protecting escape routes and buying time for evacuation. A fire door only works when it is closed. Never wedge or prop a fire door open. If a door needs to be held open for daily use, it must be fitted with an approved automatic release device that closes it when the alarm sounds. Report any fire door that will not close properly or is damaged.

params.question: You find a fire door in a corridor wedged open with a rubber doorstop. What should you do?

answers.text: Remove the wedge so the door can close, and report it if it keeps being propped open

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Fire doors only contain fire and smoke when closed. Remove the wedge and report the problem so it can be resolved safely.

answers.text: Leave it – propping it open makes it easier to move residents around

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. A wedged-open fire door cannot hold back fire and smoke, putting everyone at risk. It must be closed.

answers.text: Wedge more doors open to match, for consistency

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Definitely not. Every propped fire door is a serious hazard. Close them and report the issue.
